title: Why Would God Allow Scripture to Be Corrupted?
subtitle: The question that rebounds: divine faithfulness, human trust, and what each tradition believes God actually promised to protect
summary: After the historical evidence about textual transmission has been laid out, Muslims and Christians often turn to a more basic theological question: if the Torah and Gospel are from God, why would He allow them to be altered at all — and does the same question apply to the Quran? This article presents the Christian argument from divine faithfulness (Isaiah 40:8, Matthew 24:35), the Islamic framework distinguishing scriptures entrusted to human guardianship (Quran 5:44, 2:79) from the Quran's own direct divine guarantee (Quran 15:9), the internal Muslim debate over what "corruption" (tahrif) actually means, and the Christian counter-arguments this raises. It closes with a philosophical look at free will and divine testing, and with what evidence each side would need to strengthen its own case.

seo.metaTitle: Why Would God Allow Scripture to Be Corrupted? Islam and Christianity Compared
seo.metaDescription: If the Torah and Gospel are from God, why let them be altered? A fair look at the Christian faithfulness argument, Islam's entrustment-and-guarantee framework (Quran 15:9), and the tahrif debate.

sections.title: The Short Answer
sections.body:
After Muslims and Christians work through the historical and manuscript evidence for how the Quran and the Bible were each transmitted, the conversation often lands on a blunter theological question: if the Torah and the Gospel really came from God, why would God allow them to be altered at all? Doesn't a God who is perfectly faithful owe His revelation better protection than that? Christians raise this forcefully, pointing to verses like Isaiah 40:8 and Matthew 24:35, both of which describe God's word as something that endures no matter what happens around it.

The Quran's own answer distinguishes between two different situations rather than treating "preservation" as one fixed promise God always makes the same way. Earlier communities, in the Islamic framework, were entrusted with guarding their own scriptures — the Quran describes rabbis and scholars as answerable "by that with which they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah" (Quran 5:44) — and the Quran also accuses some individuals of writing things "with their own hands" and calling it revelation (Quran 2:79). Guarding the text, on this reading, was a human responsibility that could be dropped, and Islamic tradition holds that in places it was. The Quran describes its own situation differently: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9) — a direct divine guarantee rather than a delegated human task, tied to the claim that the Quran is God's final revelation with no future prophet left to correct any drift in it.

Christians raise a fair counter-question at exactly this point: why trust a text's own claim about its own preservation? And a second one: doesn't the same logic that indicts earlier scriptures for having any human transmission history apply just as much to the Quran, which also has one? This article lays out the Christian case for divine faithfulness, the Islamic framework of entrustment and guarantee, the classical Muslim debate over what "corruption" (tahrif) even means, and the honest, symmetrical way both traditions answer the deeper question of how God's promises interact with human freedom to fail Him.

sections.title: A Question That Comes After the Evidence
sections.body:
This question tends to surface at a specific point in an Islamic-Christian conversation: after someone has already walked through the manuscript history — the many surviving Greek New Testament manuscripts with their textual variants, the centuries it took to settle the biblical canon, the way Quranic memorization and early manuscripts relate to each other — and a Muslim has argued that the historical evidence shows meaningful change in the Bible's text over time in a way it does not for the Quran. At that point, a Christian will often step back from the details and ask the deeper question directly: if the Torah and the Gospel really are from God, why would God let that happen at all? Isn't the claim that a text was altered, all by itself, a strange thing to say about a book God supposedly wanted people to keep?

This is not a minor objection, and it deserves to be taken at full strength rather than answered with a history lesson alone, because at bottom it is not a historical question — it is a question about God's character and promises. It also, honestly, rebounds: if divine faithfulness requires that a scripture be protected from human tampering, a Muslim has to explain why that same requirement does not fall just as hard on the Quran, which also passed through human hands, memories, and manuscripts. This article tries to take both sides of that exchange seriously, in roughly the order they usually arise.

sections.title: God's Faithfulness to His Word
sections.body:
The Christian argument starts from a straightforward premise: God is not fickle, weak, or absent from history, and Scripture itself repeatedly describes God's own word as something that cannot be defeated by time, human failure, or hostile editing. The prophet Isaiah puts it starkly in the middle of a passage about how frail and temporary human life is: "The grass withers, the flower fades; but the word of our God stands forever" (Isaiah 40:8). The contrast is deliberate — everything human is compared to grass that dries up in a season, while God's own speech is placed in a completely different category, one that does not fade with time.

Jesus makes what many Christians read as an even stronger claim about his own words specifically, in the middle of a discourse about the end of the age: "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away." (Matthew 24:35). Read at face value, this places the durability of Jesus' own teaching above the durability of the physical universe itself. For Christians who hold this text to be trustworthy testimony about what Jesus actually said, it is difficult to square that promise with the idea that his teaching, or the Scriptures that record it, could simply be lost, garbled, or overwritten by later scribes without God doing anything to stop it.

Put together, these verses ground a coherent theological argument: a God who is powerful enough to create the universe and who promises that His word endures forever should be understood to have both the ability and the will to protect that word's actual content, not merely its underlying "message" in some vaguer sense. On this reading, the idea that the text supposedly carrying God's message was significantly altered by human beings, without God intervening, sits uneasily next to God's own stated commitment to His word's endurance. This is the objection at its strongest, and it deserves an answer that engages the actual theological claim, not just the manuscript evidence.

sections.title: Entrusted to Guard, and Failing the Trust
sections.body:
The Islamic framework answers this question by first distinguishing between two very different kinds of relationship a community can have with its scripture: being given a book to guard, versus being given a guarantee that the book cannot be lost. The Quran describes earlier scripture in the first category. Speaking of the Torah, it says: "Indeed, We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light. The prophets who submitted [to Allah] judged by it for the Jews, as did the rabbis and scholars by that with which they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses thereto. So do not fear the people but fear Me, and do not exchange My verses for a small price. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the disbelievers." (Quran 5:44). On the standard Muslim reading, this verse places responsibility for guarding the Torah's content on the human scholars and rabbis who received and transmitted it — they are described as entrusted with it, not as passive recipients of an automatic divine seal that made tampering impossible regardless of what they did.

The Quran goes further, accusing some individuals of actively breaking that trust rather than simply failing to keep it through neglect: "So woe to those who write the "scripture" with their own hands, then say, "This is from Allah," in order to exchange it for a small price. Woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn." (Quran 2:79). Read together, these verses present a specific claim: that guarding earlier revelation was a delegated human responsibility, and that at least some of the people entrusted with it did not meet that responsibility — whether through misinterpretation, selective transmission, or, on the reading many classical Muslim scholars held, through actually writing things and attaching God's name to them.

This matters for the theological question at hand because it reframes what "God allowing corruption" would even mean in the Islamic framework. God did not promise, on this reading, that the Torah and Gospel's wording would be automatically protected regardless of human conduct; He entrusted communities with that task and held them accountable for the outcome, the same way the Quran elsewhere holds individuals accountable for other moral and religious failures. Failing to guard something one was trusted with is, in the Quran's own moral vocabulary, a human failure — not evidence that God is either powerless or unfaithful.

sections.title: A Different Promise for the Final Revelation
sections.body:
Having described earlier scripture as entrusted to human guardians, the Quran describes its own situation in different terms entirely. Rather than delegating the task, God states that He personally undertakes it: "Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian." (Quran 15:9). The grammar itself marks the shift — the verse uses an emphatic construction ("Indeed, it is We") that Muslim exegetes have long read as underscoring that this guardianship is not shared with, or delegated to, any human community, unlike the Torah's guardianship, which the Quran describes as belonging to "the rabbis and scholars" in 5:44.

Islamic theology ties this different arrangement to a specific claim about religious history: that prophethood itself functioned, across the earlier history described in the Quran and the Bible alike, as the mechanism God used to correct communities that had drifted from His message. A new prophet could be sent to restate, clarify, or correct what earlier generations had lost, misapplied, or altered — part of why, in the Islamic framework, so many prophets appear across the biblical narrative rather than just one. Muhammad, in Islamic belief, is the final prophet, after whom Muslims hold there will be no successor sent to correct any future drift in the message. If that claim is true, then a delegated, humanly-guarded scripture with no future corrective mechanism would leave the final message permanently exposed in a way earlier messages were not — which Islamic theology presents as the reason this one revelation needed, and received, a direct divine guarantee rather than a delegated one.

This is a coherent internal logic: earlier communities were trusted with guardianship because more prophets would come to correct any failure; the final community was given God's own guarantee because no further prophet would come after it. Whether that internal logic is persuasive depends on prior claims — that Muhammad is in fact the final prophet, and that Quran 15:9 is in fact a reliable divine guarantee rather than a human author's confident assertion about his own book — both of which are contested by non-Muslims and examined further below.

sections.title: How Corrupted, Exactly? A Debate Inside Islamic Scholarship
sections.body:
It is worth pausing on a detail often skipped in popular Muslim-Christian debate: Muslim scholars themselves have never spoken with one voice about what "tahrif" (corruption) of the Torah and Gospel actually means, or how far it goes. A detailed academic survey of classical Islamic theology on this question, by Ramazan Biçer ("The Alteration of the Sacred Books According to the Islamic Theologians," RAIS Conference Proceedings, June 2020), traces two competing readings across the classical tradition: tahrif al-lafz (alteration of the actual wording of the text) and tahrif al-ma'na, sometimes called tahrif manawi (distortion of meaning through false interpretation, while the wording itself stays intact).

Fakhr al-Din al-Razi (d. 1209), the influential jurist and Quran commentator whose Tafsir al-Kabir remains one of the most cited works of classical Quranic exegesis, addresses this directly when discussing the verses that accuse the People of the Book of tahrif. According to Biçer's survey of that commentary, al-Razi lists several possible senses of the term, including outright substitution of words, but singles out attributing a false interpretation to the text (al-ta'wilat al-batila) as, in his own assessment, the best explanation of what the Quran means by tahrif — that is, tahrif al-ma'na rather than tahrif al-lafz. The same survey finds Abu Mansur al-Maturidi (d. 944), another foundational Sunni theologian, leaning the same direction: toward reading tahrif primarily as misinterpretation rather than physical alteration of the text. Other scholars in the broader tradition, including the ninth-century Zaydi imam al-Qasim al-Rassi, likewise framed the Christian error as a matter of failing to correctly understand their own scriptures rather than possessing a physically falsified text, while later scholars, including Ibn Hazm (d. 1064), argued more forcefully for textual corruption itself (tahrif al-nass).

This internal diversity matters for a fair presentation of the question. It means the claim "the Quran teaches that Jews and Christians physically altered the wording of their scriptures" is not simply "the Islamic view" in any uncontested sense — it is one reading among others that respected classical scholars have held, alongside a competing reading, associated with figures such as al-Razi and al-Maturidi, that the Quran's charge of tahrif is best understood as accusing earlier communities of misinterpreting or misapplying scripture whose wording remained basically intact. Both readings remain live within Islamic scholarship today, and which one a given Muslim scholar or apologist reaches for often shapes how sharply they press the "corruption" argument against the Bible's transmission history.

sections.title: The Christian Response, at Full Strength
sections.body:
Christian theology and scholarship offer several responses to the Islamic framework above, and they deserve to be presented with the same seriousness given to the Islamic case.

The first response leans directly on the internal Muslim debate just described. If respected classical scholars such as al-Razi and al-Maturidi read the Quran's own charge of tahrif as being about misinterpretation rather than textual alteration, then Christians can argue that even by Islamic scholarship's own internal standards, the Quran does not clearly convict the Bible of having a corrupted text — only, at most, of having been misread or misapplied by some of its readers at some point. On this reading, the premise behind the objection this article addresses — "why would God allow the text itself to be altered" — may rest on a stronger reading of tahrif than the Quran itself requires, since a real and respected strand of Islamic exegesis, not merely modern Christian apologetics, treats tahrif as primarily interpretive.

The second response is the reliability argument, made independently of how tahrif is read. Christian textual critics — including scholars such as Bruce M. Metzger and, from an evangelical apologetic angle, F. F. Bruce — argue that the number and antiquity of surviving New Testament manuscripts allow scholars to reconstruct a text substantially identical to the original, with the many documented variants overwhelmingly affecting spelling, word order, or minor wording rather than core doctrine. On this view, God's promise that His word "stands forever" (Isaiah 40:8) does not require that every handwritten copy across centuries of hand-copying be flawlessly identical; it requires that the message God intended to convey survive intact and recoverable, and textual criticism itself is offered as the evidence that it did. Some manuscript variation, on this argument, is compatible with — and even expected within — a text that God has, in the sense that actually matters, preserved well enough.

The third response is the most philosophically pointed, because it turns the Islamic framework's own logic back on itself, and Muslim scholarship has generally engaged it honestly rather than dismissing it. If Quran 15:9's guarantee is what makes the Quran trustworthy where the Bible supposedly is not, a Christian can reasonably ask: why believe that particular verse's claim about itself? A text asserting its own perfect preservation is not, by itself, proof that the assertion is true — every scripture that makes strong claims about itself faces some version of this question. The honest answer, from the Islamic side, is not that Quran 15:9 can be independently verified by some standard entirely external to Islamic revelation; it is a claim of faith, exactly as the claims in Isaiah 40:8 and Matthew 24:35 are claims of faith for Christians. What can be examined without simply assuming the conclusion is the historical record each text actually left behind — the manuscripts, the memorization networks, the compilation history — which is precisely the ground this library's preservation-branch articles were written to examine in detail, rather than settling the question by appeal to either text's self-description alone.

sections.title: Free Will and the Trust God Places in People
sections.body:
Underneath the exegetical and historical arguments sits a shared philosophical commitment that both traditions affirm, even though they apply it to different conclusions. Both Islam and Christianity hold that God gave human beings genuine freedom, including the freedom to fail moral and religious trusts God places in them — and that this freedom is not a flaw in creation but a necessary feature of it, since forced obedience would not be the kind of relationship with God either tradition holds He desires. Both traditions also hold that God tests people, sometimes precisely by placing something valuable in their hands and observing what they do with it, and that God's own faithfulness is not disproven by human beings failing tests He allows them to face.

Where Islam and Christianity diverge is not on whether God permits human failure — both say He does — but on which specific failures each believes actually occurred, and where the line was drawn between what was entrusted to human guardianship and what God guaranteed directly. Islamic theology holds that the trust to guard the Torah and Gospel's wording was placed in the hands of the communities that received them, and that this trust, in at least some places, was not kept, while the final revelation was placed under God's own direct guarantee precisely because no further corrective prophet would follow it. Christian theology holds, by contrast, that God's covenant faithfulness extends to preserving the substance and message of His revealed word across the very same history Muslims describe as compromised, whether that confidence is grounded, as it is for many Catholic and Orthodox Christians, in a Spirit-guided church community carrying the message forward, or, as it is for many Protestants and evangelicals holding to sola scriptura, in the demonstrated textual reliability of the transmitted manuscripts themselves.

Neither position requires denying free will, and neither requires denying that God tests His creatures. What each tradition denies is that God's own promises were broken by the specific failures it believes did or did not happen to its own central text — which means the real disagreement, once the historical and exegetical arguments are laid on the table, is less about whether humans can fail a divine trust and more about the historical question of whether, and where, they actually did.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions
sections.body:
One common misconception is that this question only cuts one way, forcing Christians onto the defensive while leaving Islam untouched. In fact, as this article has shown, the same question of "why would God allow His word to be altered by human hands" rebounds directly onto the Quran's own claim in 15:9, since that claim is also a textual self-description that can only be checked against a real transmission history rather than accepted as self-certifying. Fairness requires holding both traditions to the same standard: a scripture's claim about its own preservation is a starting point for historical investigation in both cases, not a substitute for it.

A second misconception is that "tahrif" straightforwardly means, and has always meant, that Jews and Christians physically forged their scriptures word for word. As the classical debate between tahrif al-lafz and tahrif al-ma'na shows, this has never been the only Islamic reading, and influential scholars such as al-Razi and al-Maturidi leaned toward the more modest interpretive reading. Presenting "the Islamic position" as uniformly the harshest possible reading of tahrif overstates the tradition's actual internal diversity.

A third misconception, from the Christian side, is that pointing to New Testament manuscript variants is equivalent to admitting the text was corrupted in the way the Quran describes earlier scripture as corrupted. Mainstream Christian textual criticism, including scholarship produced by Christian scholars themselves, generally holds that documented manuscript variants are real but overwhelmingly minor, and that they are evidence of an ordinary, well-documented hand-copying process rather than of deliberate falsification of the kind Quran 2:79 describes. Conflating "some copying variation exists" with "the text was corrupted by design" flattens a distinction both sides ought to preserve.

sections.title: What Would It Take to Settle This?
sections.body:
This article does not declare a winner, because the disagreement rests on a genuine difference in what each tradition believes God actually promised, and that difference cannot be resolved by evidence about manuscripts alone — such evidence only shows what happened to a text's wording, not what God promised regarding it in the first place. What can be said honestly is what each side would need to show to make its own case stronger, on its own terms.

A Muslim case for the Islamic framework would be strengthened by showing, in detail and against the strongest counter-evidence, that the actual transmission history of the Quran — the interlocking record of memorization and early manuscript compilation examined in this library's preservation-branch articles — matches the specific, verbatim guarantee Quran 15:9 describes, rather than treating that guarantee as self-evidently true because the Quran states it. A Christian case for the Christian framework would be strengthened by showing, with comparable historical rigor, that the New Testament's documented manuscript variants really do fall short of touching the substance of the gospel message, in line with what textual critics such as Metzger and Bruce argue, and by engaging directly with the internal Muslim debate over tahrif al-ma'na rather than treating "the Quran says our scripture was corrupted" as a single, uncontested Islamic claim.

Both sides, in other words, would need to do the same kind of work: test a scripture's claim about itself against the actual, checkable history of how that scripture reached the present day, rather than resolving the question by appeal to the strength of the claim alone. That is the spirit in which this library's preservation-branch articles were written, and it is the same spirit this article has tried to apply to the theological question of why God would allow — or not allow — His word to be altered in the first place.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does the Quran say the Bible's text itself was physically changed, or just misinterpreted?
A: Classical Islamic scholarship is genuinely divided on this. Some scholars read the Quran's charge of tahrif (Quran 2:79, 5:44, and related verses) as accusing earlier communities of literally altering wording (tahrif al-lafz). Others, including influential commentators such as Fakhr al-Din al-Razi and Abu Mansur al-Maturidi, read it primarily as misinterpretation of scripture whose wording remained largely intact (tahrif al-ma'na). Both readings have real standing within the Islamic tradition.

Q: If God guarantees the Quran in 15:9, why couldn't God have made the same guarantee for the Torah and Gospel?
A: The Islamic answer is that revelation was structured differently across history: earlier scriptures were followed by further prophets who could correct any drift, so their communities were entrusted with guardianship rather than given an unconditional divine guarantee, while the Quran, as the final revelation with no successor prophet to come, is described as directly guarded by God. Whether this explanation is convincing depends on accepting the prior claim that Muhammad is the final prophet, which is itself a matter of faith commitment rather than something the argument can prove on its own.

Q: Isn't it circular for Muslims to use Quran 15:9 to prove the Quran was preserved?
A: Taken alone, yes — a text's claim about its own reliability cannot prove that claim by itself, and this article treats that as an honest problem rather than explaining it away. Muslims typically respond by pointing past the verse itself to the historical record of memorization and manuscript transmission as the actual evidence, which is exactly the kind of evidence this library's preservation-branch articles examine.

Q: Do Christians believe every copy of every Bible manuscript is word-for-word identical?
A: No mainstream Christian scholar claims this. Textual critics openly document many manuscript variants across the New Testament's transmission history. The Christian argument is not that no variation exists, but that the variation which does exist is minor enough, in the judgment of scholars such as Bruce M. Metzger and F. F. Bruce, that the substance of the message God intended has still been reliably preserved.

Q: Does raising this question count as an attack on either faith?
A: It shouldn't be treated that way. Both Christians and Muslims take their scripture's reliability seriously enough to have produced centuries of careful scholarship examining exactly this question from the inside, including Muslim scholars debating tahrif's meaning among themselves and Christian textual critics cataloguing known manuscript variants. Engaging the question in that spirit, rather than as a rhetorical trap, is this article's aim.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body: The works below represent both the Islamic scholarship on tahrif discussed in this article and Christian scholarship on New Testament transmission, so readers can examine the primary arguments on both sides rather than relying on this article's summary alone.

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَا ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ فِيهَا هُدًۭى وَنُورٌۭ ۚ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلرَّبَّٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ بِمَا ٱسْتُحْفِظُوا۟ مِن كِتَٰبِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ شُهَدَآءَ ۚ فَلَا تَخْشَوُا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ وَٱخْشَوْنِ وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَٰتِى ثَمَنًۭا قَلِيلًۭا ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَٰفِرُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Indeed, We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light. The prophets who submitted [to Allah] judged by it for the Jews, as did the rabbis and scholars by that with which they were entrusted of the Scripture of Allah, and they were witnesses thereto. So do not fear the people but fear Me, and do not exchange My verses for a small price. And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed - then it is those who are the disbelievers.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَوَيْلٌۭ لِّلَّذِينَ يَكْتُبُونَ ٱلْكِتَٰبَ بِأَيْدِيهِمْ ثُمَّ يَقُولُونَ هَٰذَا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَشْتَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًۭا قَلِيلًۭا ۖ فَوَيْلٌۭ لَّهُم مِّمَّا كَتَبَتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَوَيْلٌۭ لَّهُم مِّمَّا يَكْسِبُونَ
quranVerses.translation: So woe to those who write the "scripture" with their own hands, then say, "This is from Allah," in order to exchange it for a small price. Woe to them for what their hands have written and woe to them for what they earn.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا ٱلذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ لَحَٰفِظُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Indeed, it is We who sent down the Qur'an and indeed, We will be its guardian.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: The grass withers, the flower fades; but the word of our God stands forever.”
Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away.

furtherReading.title: The Alteration of the Sacred Books According to the Islamic Theologians
furtherReading.author: Ramazan Biçer
furtherReading.note: An academic survey of classical Islamic theologians — including al-Razi and al-Maturidi — and their competing readings of tahrif (textual vs. interpretive corruption), cited directly in this article's discussion of the internal Muslim debate.

furtherReading.title: The New Testament Documents: Are They Reliable?
furtherReading.author: F. F. Bruce
furtherReading.note: A classic evangelical Christian defense of the New Testament's transmission reliability, representing the textual-reliability response discussed in this article.

furtherReading.title: The Text of the New Testament: Its Transmission, Corruption, and Restoration
furtherReading.author: Bruce M. Metzger
furtherReading.note: A standard, widely-cited scholarly introduction to New Testament textual criticism, written by a respected Christian biblical scholar.

furtherReading.title: An Introduction to the Sciences of the Qur'aan
furtherReading.author: Yasir Qadhi
furtherReading.note: An accessible overview, from within Islamic scholarship, of the traditional sciences developed to verify the Quran's transmission, relevant to evaluating the guarantee described in Quran 15:9.
